nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving blackwater streams, peat swamps, and densely vegetated floodplain pools with tannin-stained, soft, acidic water and leaf-litter substrate

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java moss, flame moss, floating plants)
leaf litter (Indian almond/catappa leaves) for tannins and refuge
driftwood to lower pH and add visual complexity
dark substrate to enhance coloration and reduce fish stress

diet.notes: Micropredator requiring tiny foods — live or frozen micro-fare such as Moina, baby brine shrimp, micro worms (Panagrellus), Daphnia, vinegar eels, and infusoria. Accepts high-quality micro-pellets (0.3–0.5 mm) and finely powdered flake as supplemental feeding. Mouth is very small; standard flake or pellets are too large. Varied diet enhances coloration and conditioning for breeding.

goodWith: other Boraras species (B. brigittae, B. urophthalmoides)
small Rasbora species of similar size
avoidWith: large or boisterous fish that may outcompete for food
fish large enough to consume Boraras as prey

summary: The Three-Spotted Dwarf Rasbora (Boraras micros) is one of the smallest aquarium fish in the world, reaching only around 2 cm as an adult. Native to blackwater streams in Thailand, it requires soft, acidic, tannin-rich water and accepts only tiny live or frozen micro-foods. Peaceful and shrimp-safe, it suits well-planted nano tanks.
careNotes: The Three-Spotted Dwarf Rasbora is among the smallest cyprinids kept in the aquarium hobby, with adults rarely exceeding 2 cm. The species originates from soft, acidic blackwater habitats in Thailand, and replicating these conditions is essential for long-term health and natural behaviour. Water should be very soft (general hardness 1–6 dGH, carbonate hardness 0–3 dKH) and acidic (pH 4.0–6.5); hard or alkaline tap water should be pre-treated using reverse osmosis or aged peat filtration. Adding Indian almond (catappa) leaves and driftwood leaches tannins into the water column, lowering pH, releasing humic acids, and closely approximating the species’ native biotope. A dark substrate and subdued lighting are strongly recommended, as bright conditions tend to cause the fish to hide and lose their vivid pigmentation. Filtration must provide gentle flow; a sponge filter is ideal, since standard power filters create too much current and pose a risk of trapping or injuring these tiny fish. A minimum group of 8 individuals is advisable to bring out shoaling behaviour and reduce individual stress; larger groups of 15–20 produce more cohesive, impressive displays. Because the species is extremely small, tankmate selection is critical — only similarly sized, peaceful fish and dwarf shrimp should be housed alongside them. Boraras micros is generally considered shrimp-safe: adult dwarf shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina) are not at risk, though newly moulted juveniles may occasionally be investigated. The species is intermediate in difficulty due to its strict water chemistry requirements, its very small mouth necessitating specialised feeding, and its sensitivity to poor water quality.
breedingNotes: The Three-Spotted Dwarf Rasbora is an egg-scatterer that spawns among fine-leaved plants and moss. No parental care is provided, and adults will eat the eggs if left in the tank, so removing them promptly after spawning is essential. Conditioning breeding adults on live micro-prey such as Moina and micro worms for one to two weeks markedly improves spawning success. Spawning is typically triggered by a small water change of around 10–15%, using slightly cooler water that is very soft and acidic (pH 5.0–5.5, general hardness at or below 2 dGH). Eggs hatch in approximately 24 to 48 hours. The fry are extremely tiny and require infusoria or commercial liquid fry food for the first week before they are large enough to accept micro worms and baby brine shrimp nauplii. A dedicated breeding tank of 15–20 litres, densely planted with Java moss, is generally considered the most reliable setup. Breeding is rated moderate in difficulty due to the precision required in water chemistry and the demands of rearing such small fry.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Boraras micros
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (~2 cm), temperature range 22–28°C, pH 4.0–6.5, hardness 1–6 dGH, native range (Thailand), blackwater biotope, diet (micropredator), compatibility, and breeding notes. Tank minimum and decor guidance cross-checked against FishBase and Aquarium Co-Op.

additional.name: FishBase — Boraras micros
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 1.6 cm SL, approximately 2 cm TL), taxonomy (Cyprinidae, Cypriniformes), native range (Thailand — Chao Phraya and Mae Klong drainages), temperature (22–28°C), and diet classification (omnivore, micropredator of zooplankton).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Chili Rasbora and Nano Fish Care
additional.notes: Supplemental source for general Boraras husbandry, nano-tank setup recommendations, water parameter guidance for soft-water Southeast Asian fish, and compatible tankmate lists. No species-specific Boraras micros page available; general Boraras/nano-fish guidance applied.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Boraras species guide
additional.notes: Supplemental source covering the Boraras genus, care requirements, distinguishing B. micros from B. brigittae by the three-spot pattern (two lateral spots plus one near the caudal peduncle), blackwater biotope requirements, group size recommendations (10+), and breeding overview.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Boraras micros species profile
additional.notes: Supplemental source confirming three-spot identification, Thai origin (Chao Phraya drainage), soft-acidic blackwater requirements, micropredator diet, shrimp compatibility, and nano biotope tank suitability. Breeding notes (egg-scattering, Java moss, fry rearing) confirmed against SeriouslyFish primary.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterers that spawn in dense Java moss. Adults provide no parental care and will consume the eggs, so they must be removed after spawning. Very soft, acidic water and conditioning on live micro-prey are needed to trigger spawning reliably. Fry are extremely tiny and require infusoria for the first week of life.
